---
title: "ACS Data & Mapping Examples"
author: "Jessica Klein, Census Bureau"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output: html_document
---

<a href="https://github.com/JessMK/acs_mapping" class="github-corner" aria-label="View source on GitHub"><svg width="80" height="80" viewBox="0 0 250 250" style="fill:#205493; color:#fff; position: absolute; top: 0; border: 0; right: 0;" aria-hidden="true"><path d="M0,0 L115,115 L130,115 L142,142 L250,250 L250,0 Z"/><path d="M128.3,109.0 C113.8,99.7 119.0,89.6 119.0,89.6 C122.0,82.7 120.5,78.6 120.5,78.6 C119.2,72.0 123.4,76.3 123.4,76.3 C127.3,80.9 125.5,87.3 125.5,87.3 C122.9,97.6 130.6,101.9 134.4,103.2" fill="currentColor" style="transform-origin: 130px 106px;" class="octo-arm"/><path d="M115.0,115.0 C114.9,115.1 118.7,116.5 119.8,115.4 L133.7,101.6 C136.9,99.2 139.9,98.4 142.2,98.6 C133.8,88.0 127.5,74.4 143.8,58.0 C148.5,53.4 154.0,51.2 159.7,51.0 C160.3,49.4 163.2,43.6 171.4,40.1 C171.4,40.1 176.1,42.5 178.8,56.2 C183.1,58.6 187.2,61.8 190.9,65.4 C194.5,69.0 197.7,73.2 200.1,77.6 C213.8,80.2 216.3,84.9 216.3,84.9 C212.7,93.1 206.9,96.0 205.4,96.6 C205.1,102.4 203.0,107.8 198.3,112.5 C181.9,128.9 168.3,122.5 157.7,114.1 C157.9,116.9 156.7,120.9 152.7,124.9 L141.0,136.5 C139.8,137.7 141.6,141.9 141.8,141.8 Z" fill="currentColor" class="octo-body"/></svg></a><style>.github-corner:hover .octo-arm{animation:octocat-wave 560ms ease-in-out}@keyframes octocat-wave{0%,100%{transform:rotate(0)}20%,60%{transform:rotate(-25deg)}40%,80%{transform:rotate(10deg)}}@media (max-width:500px){.github-corner:hover .octo-arm{animation:none}.github-corner .octo-arm{animation:octocat-wave 560ms ease-in-out}}</style>

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE)
options(tigris_use_cache = TRUE)
```

Mental Model for working with the Census API

Every tidycensus call is a structured API query that returns a data frame.

- Every Census API call is a dataset + a geography + variables + filters = a table
- Every Census map is a data call + shape file + join = visualization

We’ll repeat this pattern across multiple datasets.

For more geography functions/options, see the [tidycensus documentation](https://walker-data.com/census-r/an-introduction-to-tidycensus.html#geography-and-variables-in-tidycensus).

To set up your working session, load the below packages, which were all installed in the setup file.

```{r packages}
# For Census Data

library(tidycensus) # ACS, Decennial 
library(censusapi) # everything else  

# For Census ShapeFiles

library(tigris)

# For working with the data and visualization

library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(ggplot2)

# For interactive maps

library(mapview)

# For map/visual formatting and composition

library(patchwork)

# For number formatting helpers

library(scales)

# Disable scientific notation
options(scipen = 999)
```

First, lets take a look at the available datasets.

```{r apis}
apis <- listCensusApis()   # Get general information about available datasets

head(apis[ , -9], 30) #1788 api options


# Lets filter it down to ACS related only

ACS_api <- apis %>%
    filter(grepl("ACS", name, ignore.case = TRUE))  # 273 ACS related API options

table(ACS_api$vintage) # We have data available since 2004
```

Next, lets look at the available variables within a dataset.

```{r variabes}
ACS5_24_vars <- load_variables(2024, "acs5")

tail(ACS5_24_vars, 20)

# Additional examples on how to use this function:

ACS1_23_vars <- load_variables(2023, "acs1")
ACS1_13_vars <- load_variables(2013, "acs1")
ACS5_22_vars <- load_variables(2022, "acs5")
```

Example 1: Mapping State Population Estimates from the ACS

Goals: Retrieve ACS 1-year 2024 population estimates, join them to state boundaries, and create a choropleth map using ggplot2.

- See additional geography options [here](http://api.census.gov/data/2024/acs/acs1/geography.json)
- See additional variable options [here](http://api.census.gov/data/2024/acs/acs1/variables.json)

```{r state-pop}
## Step 1: Data call to the Census API

pop_acs <- get_acs(
    geography = "state",
    variables = c("B01001_001E",    # Total Population by State
                  "B01001_002E",    # Male Population by State
                  "B01001_026E"),   # Female Population by State
    year = 2024, 
    survey= "acs1",
    show_call = TRUE)                 # show the URL 


## Step 2: Get your ShapeFile from tigris

states_sf <- states(year = 2024)


## Step 3: Join the data and ShapeFile

map_pop_acs <- left_join(states_sf, 
                         pop_acs, 
                         by = "GEOID")


## Step 4: Visualization, create a map

ggplot(map_pop_acs) +
    geom_sf(aes(fill = estimate)) +
    scale_fill_viridis_c(direction = -1) +
    labs(title = "Total Population Distribution Across the United States",
         caption = "Source: American Community Survey 1-year Estimate 2024")

# note that this is the standard view, but this is not the Census standard view, we will see a geography shift in Example 3
```

Workflow: ACS Data → State Shapefile → Join → Map

### Why Does Alaska Look So Far Away?

This map uses the original state geometries provided by the Census Bureau. Notice that Alaska and Hawaii appear in their true geographic locations.

Many cartographic products shift Alaska and Hawaii closer to the continental United States. The purpose of this shift is not to change the data, but to improve map readability and make better use of available space. We begin with displaying the original geometries so you can see the source data before introducing cartographic adjustments in later examples.


Example 2: Creating Interactive Population Maps with mapview

Goal: Create an interactive map that allows the option to zoom, pan, and inspect population estimates for individual states.

```{r acs-pop}
## Step 1: Data call to the Census API

pop_acs_wide <- get_acs(
    geography = "state",
    variables = c("B01001_001E",    # Total Population by State
                  "B01001_002E",    # Male Population by State
                  "B01001_026E"),   # Female Population by State
    year = 2024, 
    survey= "acs1",
    output = "wide",
    show_call = TRUE)  

## Step 2: Join your shapefile and your data

map_pop_acs_wide <- left_join(states_sf, 
                              pop_acs_wide, 
                              by = "GEOID")

## Step 3: Visualize using mapview

mapview((map_pop_acs_wide %>% 
             select(c("NAME.y", 
                      "REGION",
                      "B01001_001E",
                      "B01001_002E",
                      "B01001_026E")) %>%
             dplyr::rename(State = NAME.y,
                    Region = REGION,
                    Total = B01001_001E,
                    'Total Males' = B01001_002E,
                    'Total Females' = B01001_026E) %>%
             mutate(Region = case_when(
                 Region == "1" ~ "Northeast",
                 Region == "2" ~ "Midwest", 
                 Region == "3" ~ "South",
                 Region == "4" ~ "West"))), 
        zcol = "Region", 
        layer.name = "Region")
```

Workflow: ACS Data → Wide Format → State Shapefile → Join → Interactive Map


Example 3: ACS Population (States) with shifted geometry

Goal: Retrieve ACS data with geometry included, apply a geometry shift, and create a state-level population map.

```{r state-acs-pop}
## Step 1: Data call to the Census API with geometry included, and shift it to a Census standard

pop_acs_geo <- get_acs(
    geography = "state",
    variables = "B01001_001",
    geometry = TRUE,   # bring the geometry in during the data call, tidycensus makes this possible
    year = 2024,      
    survey= "acs1",
    show_call = TRUE) %>% 
    shift_geometry()  # shift_geometry gives us the census standard view


## Step 2: Visualization, we don't need to get the shapefile since we have geometry

ggplot(pop_acs_geo) +
    geom_sf(aes(fill = estimate)) +
    scale_fill_viridis_c(direction = -1) +
    labs(title = "State Population Estimates Using Shifted Geometries",
         caption = "Source: American Community Survey 1-year Estimate 2024")
```

Workflow: ACS Data with Geometry → Shift Geometry → Map

### Why Shift State Geometries?

Alaska and Hawaii are geographically far from the continental United States. While their true locations are accurate, they leave large areas of empty space and can make national maps more difficult to read.

A geometry shift positions Alaska and Hawaii closer to the lower 48 states for display purposes. The underlying data do not change—only the visual placement of the features on the map.


Example 4: Mapping 2020 Decennial Census Population by County

Goal: Retrieve Decennial Census population data, obtain county boundaries from tigris, apply a geometry shift, and create a county-level population map.

```{r decennial}
## Step 1: Data call to the Census API

pop_dec <- get_decennial(
    geography = "county",
    variables = "P1_001N",
    year = 2020,
    show_call = TRUE)


## Step 2: ShapeFile from tigris

counties_sf_20 <- counties(year = 2020)

# Shift Alaska, Hawaii, and Puerto Rico, another way to shift the geometry
counties_sf_shifted <- shift_geometry(counties_sf_20)


## Step 3: Join the data and ShapeFile

map_pop_dec <- left_join(counties_sf_shifted, 
                         pop_dec, 
                         by = "GEOID") %>%
    filter(!is.na(NAME.y))              # no data for these territories/islands


## Step 4: Visualization, create a map

ggplot(map_pop_dec) +
    geom_sf(aes(fill = value)) +
    scale_fill_viridis_c(direction = -1) +
    labs(title = "County Population Counts from the 2020 Decennial Census",
         caption = "Source: Decennial Census 2020")
```

Workflow: Decennial Census Data → County Shapefile → Shift Geometry → Remove Territories → Join → Map


Example 5: Mapping County-Level Poverty Rates in the Mid-Atlantic Region

Goal: Retrieve ACS 5-year poverty estimates, filter to a multi-state region, apply a geometry shift, and visualize results using both static (ggplot2) and interactive (mapview) maps.

```{r poverty-county}
## Step 1: Data call to the Census API

poverty_county <- get_acs(
    geography = "county",
    variables = "B17001_002",
    state = c("MD", "VA", "PA", "DC", "DE", "WV"),
    year = 2023,
    survey = "acs5",
    show_call = TRUE)

state_counties <- poverty_county$GEOID

## Step 2: ShapeFile from tigris

counties_sf_24 <- counties(year = 2023, 
                           cb = TRUE) # cb = generalized shapefile, more details/more resources

counties_sf_24_shifted <- shift_geometry(counties_sf_24)


## Step 3: Join the data and ShapeFile

map_pov_acs5 <- counties_sf_24_shifted %>%
    filter(GEOID %in% state_counties) %>%
    left_join(poverty_county, by = c("GEOID"))
    

## Step 4: Visualization, create a map

ggplot(map_pov_acs5) +
    geom_sf(aes(fill = estimate)) +
    scale_fill_viridis_c(direction = -1) +
    labs(
        title = "County-Level Poverty Counts in the Mid-Atlantic Region",
        fill = "People",
        caption = "Source: American Community Survey 5-year Estimates 2023")

# Try mapview for an interactive view

mapview((map_pov_acs5 %>% 
            select(c("NAME.y", 
                     "NAMELSAD", 
                     "STATE_NAME",
                     "estimate",
                     "moe")) %>%
            rename('County and State' = NAME.y,
                   County = NAMELSAD,
                   State = STATE_NAME,
                   'Poverty Count Estimate' = estimate,
                   'Poverty Count Margin of Error' = moe)), 
        zcol = "Poverty Count Estimate", 
        layer.name = "Poverty Count Estimate")
```

Workflow: ACS 5-Year Data → County Shapefile → Filter Region → Shift Geometry → Join → Static Map (ggplot2) → Interactive Map (mapview)


Example 6: Mapping Bachelor's Degree Attainment Across California Census Tracts

Goal: Retrieve ACS 5-year educational attainment data, join it to California census tract boundaries, and visualize geographic variation in bachelor's degree attainment.

```{r education-tract}
## Step 1: Data call to the Census API

edu_tract <- get_acs(
    geography = "tract",
    variables = "B15003_022", # bachelor's degree
    state = "CA",
    year = 2022,
    survey = "acs5",
    show_call = TRUE)


## Step 2: ShapeFile from tigris

tracts_sf_24 <- tracts(state = "CA", 
                       year = 2022, 
                       cb = TRUE)


## Step 3: Join the data and ShapeFile

map_edu_acs5 <- tracts_sf_24 %>%
    left_join(edu_tract, by = "GEOID")


## Step 4: Visualization, create a map

ggplot(map_edu_acs5) +
    geom_sf(aes(fill = estimate), color = NA) +
    scale_fill_viridis_c(direction = -1) +
    labs(
        title = "Bachelor's Degree Attainment Across California Census Tracts",
        fill = "Estimate",
        caption = "Source: American Community Survey 5-year Estimates 2022")

# Try mapview for an interactive visualization

mapview(map_edu_acs5 %>% 
            select(c("NAME.y", 
                     "NAMELSAD", 
                     "STATE_NAME",
                     "estimate",
                     "moe")) %>%
            rename('County and State' = NAME.y,
                   County = NAMELSAD,
                   State = STATE_NAME,
                   'Bachelors Degree Attainment Estimate' = estimate,
                   'Bachelors Degree Attainment Margin of Error' = moe), 
        zcol = "Bachelors Degree Attainment Estimate", 
        layer.name = "Bachelors Degree Attainment Estimate")
```

Workflow: ACS 5-Year Data → California Tract Shapefile → Join → Map

We now have ACS 5-year estimates for residents with a bachelor's degree at the census tract level throughout California.


Example 7: Mapping Median Household Income with Built-In Geometry

Goal: Retrieve ACS median household income estimates with geometry included and create a state-level choropleth map using ggplot2.

```{r income-state}
## Step 1: Data call to the Census API

income_state <- get_acs(
    geography = "state",
    variables = "B19013_001",
    year = 2024,
    survey = "acs1",
    geometry = TRUE,             # no need to get the shapefile, we have the geometry
    show_call = TRUE) %>%
    shift_geometry()

## Step 2: Visualization, create a map

ggplot(income_state) +
    geom_sf(aes(fill = estimate)) +
    scale_fill_viridis_c(direction = -1, labels = scales::dollar) +
    labs(title = "United States Income by State", 
         subtitle = "Geometry pulled directly",
         caption = "Source: American Community Survey 1-year Estimate 2024")
```

Workflow: ACS Data with Shifted Geometry → Map

We now have ACS 1-year estimates of median household income along with state geometries in a single dataset. Because the geometry was retrieved directly from the Census API and shifted, no separate shapefile download or join is required.


Example 8: Calculating and Mapping Poverty Rates by County

Goal: Retrieve ACS poverty estimates, calculate county-level poverty rates using a numerator and denominator, apply a geometry shift, and visualize the results using both static and interactive maps.

```{r poverty-rate-county}
## Step 1: Data call to the Census API

poverty_pct <- get_acs(
    geography = "county",
    variables = "B17001_002",     # estimate, Total:!!Income in the past 12 months below poverty level
    summary_var = "B17001_001",   # estimate denominator, Poverty Status in the Past 12 Months by Sex by Age
    year = 2024,
    survey = "acs5",
    geometry = TRUE) %>%
    shift_geometry() %>%    
    mutate(pct_poverty = percent(estimate / summary_est, accuracy = 0.1))

poverty_pct$pct_poverty_num <- as.numeric(sub("%", "", poverty_pct$pct_poverty)) / 100

## Step 2: Visualization, create a map

ggplot(poverty_pct) +
    geom_sf(aes(fill = pct_poverty_num)) +
    scale_fill_viridis_c(direction = -1, 
                         labels = scales::label_percent(accuracy = 1)) +
    labs(title = "US County-Level Percent in Poverty",
         caption = "Source: American Community Survey 5-year Estimates 2024")

# Try mapview for an interactive visualization

mapview(poverty_pct %>%
            select(c("NAME", 
                     "estimate",
                     "moe",
                     "pct_poverty",
                     "pct_poverty_num")) %>%
            rename('State' = NAME,
                   'Poverty Estimate' = estimate,
                   'Poverty Estimate Margin of Error' = moe,
                   'Percent in Poverty' = pct_poverty), 
        zcol = "pct_poverty_num", 
        layer.name = "Percent in Poverty")
```

Workflow: ACS Data with Geometry → Shift Geometry → Calculate Poverty Rate → Static Map (ggplot2) → Interactive Map (mapview)

This example demonstrates the importance of converting counts to rates and shows how finer geographic detail can reveal patterns that larger geographic units may mask.


Example 9: Comparing Educational Attainment with Side-by-Side Maps

Goal: Retrieve ACS educational attainment data with geometry included, create side-by-side maps comparing bachelor's and master's degree counts, and apply a log transformation to improve visual interpretation.

```{r degree-tract}
## Step 1: Data call to the Census API

edu_ny_geo <- get_acs(
    geography = "tract",
    state = "NY",
    variables = c(
        bachelors = "B15003_022",
        masters = "B15003_023"),
    output = "wide",
    year = 2023,
    survey = "acs5",
    geometry = TRUE,
    show_call = TRUE)

head(edu_ny_geo)  # view the wide output


## Step 2: Visualization, create a side by side map

p1 = ggplot(edu_ny_geo) +
    geom_sf(aes(fill = bachelorsE)) +
    scale_fill_viridis_c(direction = -1) +
    labs(title = "Bachelor's Degrees")

p2 = ggplot(edu_ny_geo) +
    geom_sf(aes(fill = mastersE)) +
    scale_fill_viridis_c(direction = -1) +
    labs(title = "Master's Degrees", 
         caption = "Source: American Community Survey 5-year Estimates 2023")

(p1 | p2) + plot_annotation(
  title = "Educational Attainment Across New York Census Tracts")


# Visualization with a log transformation, to bring the colors out

p3 = ggplot(edu_ny_geo) +
    geom_sf(aes(fill = bachelorsE)) +
    scale_fill_viridis_c(trans = "log10", direction = -1) +
    labs(title = "Bachelor's Degrees (Log10)")

p4 = ggplot(edu_ny_geo) +
    geom_sf(aes(fill = mastersE)) +
    scale_fill_viridis_c(trans = "log10", direction = -1) +
    labs(title = "Master's Degrees (Log10)", 
         caption = "Source: American Community Survey 5-year Estimates 2023")

(p3 | p4) + plot_annotation(
  title = "Educational Attainment Across New York Census Tracts (Log10 Scale)")
```

Workflow: ACS Data with Geometry → Pivot Wide → Create Side-by-Side Maps → Apply Log Transformation → Compare Results

We now have ACS educational attainment estimates and tract geometries for New York in a single spatial dataset. The log-transformed maps reveal patterns that were difficult to identify in the original visualizations because the color scale is no longer dominated by a small number of high-value tracts. Log transformation change how we visualize the data.


Example 10: Mapping Income and Landmarks in Washington, DC

Goal: Retrieve ACS median household income estimates with geometry included, add landmark locations from a supplemental shapefile, and create a layered map that combines socioeconomic data with geographic context.

```{r dc-landmarks}
## Step 1: Data call to the Census API

dc_income <- get_acs(
    geography = "tract",
    state = "DC",
    variables = "B19013_001",
    survey = "acs5",
    year = 2024,
    geometry = TRUE,
    show_call = TRUE)


## Step 2: ShapeFile from tigris

dc_landmarks <- landmarks(state = "DC")


## Step 3: Visualization, create a map with two layers

ggplot() +
    geom_sf(data = dc_income, aes(fill = estimate), alpha = 0.7) +
    geom_sf(data = dc_landmarks, color = "red", size = 1) +
    labs(title = "Median Household Income and Major Landmarks in Washington, DC",
         caption = "Source: American Community Survey 5-year Estimates 2024")
```

Workflow: ACS Data with Geometry → Landmark Shapefile → Overlay Layers → Map

### Demonstration Takeaway

Throughout these examples, we have used several approaches to create maps with Census data:

- Retrieving data with and without geometry
- Downloading and joining shapefiles
- Applying geometry shifts
- Calculating derived measures
- Comparing counts and rates
- Creating static and interactive visualizations
- Combining multiple geographic layers

These techniques form the foundation of many real-world demographic, economic, and spatial analysis workflows.

### Keep Exploring

The best way to build confidence with Census mapping is through experimentation. In the `examples` folder, you'll find two practice files:

- `practice_api` — practice retrieving Census data and working with geometry directly from the API.
- `practice_shapefiles` — practice downloading, joining, and visualizing geographic boundaries.

Try swapping in different Census variables, changing the geography level, creating new derived measures, or experimenting with alternative map designs. Every example in this training can be adapted to answer a different question.
